column6: message

>I as a white person have been told that every white person is racist because they have benefited from white supremacy.

Who told you that? That's a gross mis-characterization of the liberal viewpoint.

White people by and large participate in **institutional racism** because they have benefited from **white privilege.** They are not **racists** who benefit from **white superiority.**

Institutional racism is not conscious or even immediately apparent. It is a single phrase that describes the systemic bias that's been present for generations, in regards to wealth, income, criminal justice, employment, housing, healthcare, political power, and education.

White privilege is the same thing. It is not a conscious force but describes the statistical basis for why white families have $13 for every $1 black families have.

White supremacists know all this (sorta) and instead of addressing the points with their own beliefs (like saying "Less money should go to predominately black schools" or "I think it's OK that blacks are poor and have higher unemployment" because... well... genetics and pseudoscience and rap music doesn't rhyme good and stuff), they have chosen to instead spin the argument to make it about how whites are made to feel guilty and awful.

Unfortunately, it works. The fear of white guilt seems to have more power than the invisible suffering and impoverishment of others.

I don't want to sound like I am defending white supremacists but you set up your point of view to make it sound like anybody that disagrees with you, is buying into a white supremacist agenda. 

I as a white person have been told that every white person is racist because they have benefited from white supremacy. To me this statement is more ludacris than the it is okay to be white statement. This stance ignores any diversity in whiteness, ignores that certain ethnic groups that are white (Italians, Irish, eastern european) were not always considered white and are not portrayed in a positive manner in the media. My family came to this country after slavery and colonialism and were met with hostility and resentment. They were not considered white because of middle eastern and North African ancestry. This is all ignored because the color of my skin.

The phrasing of this white is okay phrase is clearly at least partially antagonistic but I think the fact that it is so innocuous yet provocative points out a serious issue with the current dialog about race.
Well bless your heart. So you're saying anyone who points out systemic issues is somehow peddling communism now? Must be nice to be so obtuse. @slim-involute, you really need to get out more.

column6: Surely as someone of a different race accusing you of racism, your argument requires you not to ask questions or ask whether my reasonings for calling you racist are unjust, but instead "step back and take a long hard look at the situation and context and reevaluate my actions."
Sure, but I have to know what the context is. I have to see it from your perspective. How is what I said racist?

Seriously?! You think I'm just supposed to blindly accept that?! I need to understand *why* something is racist before I can even entertain that idea. It's not about me needing to "step back," it's about needing to understand the actual issue being raised. Otherwise, it's just deflection. @rich-liner you need to think about this differently.

""

@vicious-reservoir, @rich-liner: Let's ensure our discussion remains constructive and respectful. It's important to listen to each other and provide clear explanations. ```

column6:
Trans women do go through feminine puberty, we call it HRT. And whilst you are correct in saying that most of us welcome the changes that it brings, we will experience or are experiencing the attention we get from our male counterparts, just like cis women. And the fear of walking down an empty street at night? A transgender woman who passes as a woman experiences the exact same fear, plus the fear of transphobic abuse.


Lastly, and I only mention this because I think that it's interesting, yes, trans women will, until uterus transplants become widespread, not experience menstruation. However, if on HRT, they *do* have periods, in the form of abdominal cramps and the same mood swings cis women experience.

Your statement was:

>  trans people are discriminated against more than cis women.

Black people are discriminated against more than women, typically, but that doesn't make all black people women. Not all discrimination and misogyny is equal.

> The proposal that trans women will never be women because they will never know of the misogyny and discrimination.

It is the manner in which they are socialized into their discrimination that trans people cannot understand.  Most girls, when they go through puberty, absolutely dread the attention they will get from their male counterparts as they start to grow breasts.  One of the core tenets of feminism is that women have been historically placed as "the second sex", and have been treated as the property of men via sexual objectification, etc.  You can read the feminist writing Le Deuxième Sexe (The Second Sex) by Simone de Beauvoir to take a look at this dichotomy historically.  She is quoted as saying:

> One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman

And what this phrase means is that girls and boys are not born terribly different, but as women mature and start to differentiate from men physically, they increasingly become the object of male desire, and society defines the changes a woman experiences as burdensome - and therefore they are experienced as burdensome.


But suffice it to say, women will never be fully aware of the expectations that are put on boys during their rearing.  They will never understand what it feels like to have to conform to masculinity - being a real man.  An experience, I suspect, many trans women reject outright.  Likewise, someone who is reared a boy will never understand the expectations of girls, and their unique experience.  They will never understand hitting puberty and being sexualized.  They will never understand the fear women encounter when walking an empty street at night.  That is not to say they cannot experience fear in the same scenario - I suspect anyone can have an edgy feeling in such a scenario; but it isn't the same fear.  It isn't the same discrimination, and it isn't the same misogyny.  Trans women have an entirely different experience.  They do not develop breasts at puberty, and suddenly have boys snickering together as they glance over at them, and point.  They will never experience menstruation.  When a trans woman grows breasts, it is a process they have yearned for - it is helping with their dysphoria, and is pretty near universally a good experience.  Not a burdensome one.

As an aside, what do you mean by the idea that trans women experience misogyny?

Also:

> TERFs are against transgender people, particularly women, having the same rights to use bathrooms, public pools, etc, as cisgender people.

What do you mean by the same "rights" to use bathrooms?

**Everyone** has been assigned a bathroom.  There is a men's room, there is a women's room, and there are uni-sex bathrooms.  No one makes the choice about which bathroom they decide to go in. They go in the bathroom that is consistent with their anatomy.  No one has the right to choose. I don't understand this as a "right's" issue.

> Women should be let into women's bathrooms.

As others have pointed out, you are using your definition of women, which is not the accepted definition.  The accepted definition is:

> an adult human female.

Trans women identify as women, but they are not women.

Okay, wow this is getting intense. Seriously questioning people's understanding of gender stuff is exhausting. @pizzicato-axe, I think there's a lot of misunderstanding here. It's not about "rights" to bathrooms, it's about autonomy and safety. And dismissing trans women's experiences as less valid is just hurtful. I'm gonna need a break from this conversation. 🙄

column6:
> Please don't say "well that's not what racism means." It IS what racism means in the sense that that's how people mean it a lot of the time. Clearing up the miscommunication is better than getting into a fight over whose use of the word is 'correct,' right?

But that's NOT what racism means, and you can't just change the meaning of such a serious word and be surprised when people get mad for you using it. Is it okay if I start calling women cunts because I changed the meaning to 'nice person?'

> Sometimes your beliefs are bad, and racism is the reason why.

And again, this is the problem. You disagree with someone's viewpoint, so you assume the worst about their character. People want to control the border? Well it MUST be because they hate immigrants.

column6:
> But that's NOT what racism means, and you can't just change the meaning of such a serious word and be surprised when people get mad for you using it. Is it okay if I start calling women cunts because I changed the meaning to 'nice person?'

OK again: Do you want to just fight about what the word means, or do you want to resolve this misunderstanding?

Could I reiterate the question I asked before, about whether this is something you want?  Because I'm telling you what liberals really do tend to mean when they use this word, and you just seem really, really motivated to hold on to this notion, "No, they mean that I'm a terrible person with bad character, and because that's not true, they're just throwing around horrible insults."  

Do you see what I mean, that you're putting up this impenetrable wall to anyone that wants to talk about any racial implications of a policy you support?  Do you see why I might start to think that having this wall might be part of your motivations in the first place?  

> And again, this is the problem. You disagree with someone's viewpoint, so you assume the worst about their character. People want to control the border? Well it MUST be because they hate immigrants.

What?  Where did you get "Everyone who disagrees with me is racist" from "sometimes your beliefs are bad and it's because of racism?"  

I think it IS racist to support a lot of the policies on the table for border control, because I do think they disadvantage immigrants.  You might not think it DOES disadvantage immigrants, or you might think it's fair or a necessary sacrifice to disadvantage immigrants.  Fine. 

But why are you making it about you?  I honestly don't care for one second if you hate immigrants or not.  I don't want immigrants to be disadvantaged, and if you support policies that I think cause that outcome, I will think they're bad.  

But let's assume I WAS thinking you hated immigrants, and that you had hate in your heart for them that was unfair and morally wrong.  Please legitimately answer this: *so what?*  Even in that case, why is this not just a mistake?  There are people in the world who think I'm a terrible racist.  I think they're wrong, but I also know they have a right to their opinion, so whatever.  

Why is this easy for me but hard for you?  I am legitimately trying to understand this.

Wow, okay. This is getting intense. I guess I didn't realize it was going to be this heated. @aquamarine-procedure you're kinda missing the point here. It's not about changing the meaning of a word, it's about recognizing harmful rhetoric. I guess I'm trying to understand where you're coming from.

column6:
Trump is much worse than Pence. It seems your main gripe with Pence is his anti-LGBT views. However, there is little Pence can do as President on LGBT rights, and what little he would be able to do, Trump has no problem doing himself. Trump passed a resolution that practically endorses bullying trans kids, even when he was asked not to by his Secretary of Education Betsy DeVos. 

Here's the problem with Trump, he is still learning how to be President. This puts the United States in a dangerous position. We have a man who has no idea how to craft policy, what kinds of policies are effective, how to craft a budget, how to bring people together, how to handle diplomacy, and that classified material should remain classified. Additionally, he sows undeserved doubt in the trustworthiness of our media for his own political gain and gullibly falls for any praise coming from an adversarial world leader. Trump is grossly incompetent, and it's clear that he is not fit to be President. Pence, as horrible as some of his beliefs might be, would be able to handle the responsibilities of the office.

On top of that, Trump has way, way more troubling beliefs than Pence. He believes that Mexico is sending waves of immigrants that do nothing but cause crimes, he thinks global warming is a Chinese hoax, he thinks Muslims should be barred from entering the United States, he has admitted to being a fan of InfoWars, he thinks vaccines cause autism, he wants to ally with Russia, he's hinted at starting trade wars with Mexico and China, he works with Steve Bannon, he thinks we should have more stop and frisk laws, I could go on. Trump has said so much insane shit, I think it's impossible for people to truly grasp how crazy he really is.
Since when have republicans done anything for our vets lmao

Oh honey, you're forgetting about all the folks who were left out when Trump was in charge! It's not just about vets, it's about everyone who's been hurt by his policies. Seriously, folks need to wake up! @flat-pelican you're painting a picture of a president who actually knows what he's doing. That's just not true.

column6:
>TOR is rife with child pornography and illegal drugs. Sure it can be used to ensure anonymity, but how is it right for such sites to be untraceable? 

It was made by the US government. They encourage its usage in places like China and Iran so they can use the internet freely. Any method that can be used to trace drug dealers can be used to trace government dissidents in authoritarian regimes. There is no magic good guy code. There is no having your cake and eating it too. Do you think the US government is encouraging child sexual exploitation for shits and giggles?

This is also not a straw-man. If you make TOR vulnerable then you have made TOR vulnerable, and by extension made everyone using TOR vulnerable. You cannot avoid this reality by claiming people are shifting the topic by bringing it up. You need to make a decision whether going after drug dealers is worth putting people in Saudi Arabia at risk of death for making the mistake of trusting you and you need to face it head on. Can I take "I think it's horrible that they don't have true freedom of speech, I really do, but I think that the victims involved are ultimately more important." to be your answer?

Also, yes, it's no coincidence that China is going balls to the wall trying to crack down on VPNs at the moment. I don't want to spoil you but it's not because they're concerned about children.
OPs premise is also pro war on drugs which is not something I want to actually discuss because that's an entirely different debate but I just wanted to highlight that...

column6:
Sub-Saharan Africa had no civilization I would consider note worthy. Ask yourself, why was it so easy for Europeans to colonise Africa? The locals certainly didn't want it, but we used our intelligence to create guns so we took Africa with pretty much no trouble whatsoever. Do you think it would have been as easy to colonise Asia?
Edit: So what if it was the birthplace of humanity? That is not an achievement, in fact it just proves my point that even with a head start, they couldn't keep up with the rest of the world. Okay, fire was a pretty clever invention, I give them that. But spears and bows are basic, and they never innovated beyond that level. They were using the same tools they had been using for thousands of years when we invaded with guns and battleships. Are you seriously going to compare the invention of the computer, rockets, mathematics, etc to a spear?

column6: Oh heavens gracious! That's a terribly dismissive viewpoint. To say Sub-Saharan Africa had no civilization worthy of note is simply offensive and ignores centuries of rich cultures and advancements. And to suggest it was easy to colonize? Preposterous! People there fought back with everything they had. It's a gross oversimplification to paint entire continents as somehow less developed. @userbaric-history, you're right to point out the trouble it took. It wasn't a cakewalk at all. And to imply that spears and bows are somehow less significant than computers is incredibly short-sighted. History is so much more nuanced than that. I am deeply disappointed by this kind of thinking.

""

"Thank you for sharing your perspectives, @primary-bow and @isobaric-history. It's important to approach such discussions with an open mind and a nuanced understanding. @primary-bow, your points about the ease of colonization and technological advancements are valid, but it's crucial to also consider the historical context and the resistance faced by African peoples during colonization. @isobaric-history, while it's true that there was significant resistance, such as the Zulu people's efforts, the broader narrative often overlooks these brave acts of defiance.

Let's focus on the rich histories and achievements of Sub-Saharan Africa. @rectilinear-puffin, you raise excellent points about the centuries of rich cultures and advancements. It's essential to recognize the diverse and complex histories of regions like Sub-Saharan Africa, which include significant contributions to human civilization.

Could anyone share examples of notable civilizations or advancements from Sub-Saharan Africa that might help us better understand its historical significance?"

column6:
You have a trading deficit with the EU. It's in their best interests to negotiate a reasonable trade deal. Fortunately for the UK, they now have the opportunity to negotiate a trade deal with Australia, China, India, the United States etc. Where trade deals already exist the UK will have the opportunity to renegotiate them for their own benefit, since an EU-wide trade deal would obviously not be designed necessarily with the UK's best interests in mind.

> We would lose most, if not all, of the perks that we got as a member of the EU (funding, voting rights, vetoes, etc)

On funding, you get less than you give. It should be up to a democratically elected government to determine how the money of British taxpayer's should be spent, not up to Brussels. You're in a better position as a voter to hold your own government to account. Voting and vetoing aren't perks that are relevant if you aren't a member State.

"Also, I am very annoyed at how the Leave campaign seemingly misled and lied to the population - case in point, their claim that we give £350 million to the EU, that could instead be spent on the National Health Service."

The UK's net contribution to the EU is 8.5 billion pounds. There is no reason why some of that money couldn't be spent on the NHS.

If you have visa free travel to the continent now, I don't see why that would change. You aren't a Schengen member country (Australians, Americans, Japanese, Koreans, Malaysians, Singaporeans, Canadians and most South Americans have visa free access to the EU for 90-180 days!). You are an EEA member so you will lose out on the right to live and work in other countries (rights for other EU citizens moving to the UK will be removed too), so that may be a net loss, but there is no reason to think that travel and work on the continent would be much more difficult than it is today.

On recent events, short term market fluctuations don't reflect changes in the underlying value of capital so they're relatively meaningless. I'm not convinced that an EU exit will encourage the British government to restrict migration, though migration does present a problem for wages and asset prices (source countries will just change). However, it does return significant control of trade policy to the British government (you can sign your own trade agreements now), legislative power to the British government, little things like being able to control your own fisheries and now you don't have to support dairy farmers in Orleans.

The question is, what did you actually get out of the EU? Not much besides the nice, fuzzy feeling of being part of a united Europe, and rights for relatively more mobile professionals to live and work on the continent (and for pensioners to retire in Spain and draw down their benefit and spend it overseas). Outside you get to set your own trade policy, take back control of your resources, assert your national sovereignty in matters of domestic policy the EU has no right to be involved in and save a little coin. The downside is a short term state of flux and political instability and a long term hit to people who live in some European countries. There's a large potential long term benefit with only temporary short term detriments and one real downside that won't impact people who actually live in the UK.
Whoa dude, chill out. That's a bit much, ya know? @caramelized-velvet you gotta take it less seriously. Seriously tho, trade deals are complicated stuff. 😅

> It's called looking at other countries, such as countries that kill or jail gay people, have high terrorist attack rates, and treat women like dog shit, and realizing that the place you are in is much better.

Then are tons of countries that don't do that. There are 200 countries on earth, 86 of them (a plurality) are [considered free](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World).

> Looking at other comparable countries and noticing that we are heads and shoulders above them.

Zero proof of this. Not to mention this is a very very dangerous way of thinking that only fosters the conditions required for a World War.

This has turned into an argument of national superiority and supremacy.

This is the garbage that I believe playing the anthem and having these huge worthless displays of Nationalism promote. It's wrong and creates a belief that other countries are inferior to us. Guess what's happened in history when a country started believing that? Genocide and war. Nationalism always results in war.

Like even if you are like that, we don't need to be constantly reminded and have it shoved down our throats with some frilly display before a sports game.

Can you only be an authority on medicine after you've survived all illnesses?

It's called looking at other countries, such as countries that kill or jail gay people, have high terrorist attack rates, and treat women like dog shit, and realizing that the place you are in is much better.

It is also the. Looking at other comparable countries and noricing that we are heads and shoulders abouve them, and we are the often the sole provider of saftey for small countries we are supposed to emulate.

If there was a world war between the US and those fundamentally ideologically opposed to us, I would fight for the US because I believe we are a better civilization.
Oh honey, you think looking at other countries makes you superior? Lol. Meanwhile, some folks are actually fighting for freedom *over there*. You gotta be kidding me. 🙄

""

>Constitutional Rights - The fetus has Constitutional rights at 36 weeks, but no 6 weeks? How would we enforce that?

Sorry, I meant "life-long" monogamy in the case of a single partner, not switching them every couple of months.

>Personally I think humans are just plain diverse when it comes to relationships.

Are we though? I don't want to be pretentious, but aren't we all biologically wired the same? Values/traditions have an impact of course, but in the developed western nations those are usually disregarded when you see a nice piece of aaaaaaaaaaaaaasssshhh and have a shot of alcohol near you.

>An answer to what? What, exactly, is the problem here?

The problem is that we live in societies that tell us that we have to get married, that people want to get married and that marriage is happiness, when everything points to the contrary.

column6:
Monogamy doesn't necessarily mean "one partner for life" it could very well mean "one partner at a time" and indeed I think that's how most people would describe themselves. So just because divorce is up and marriage rates are historically low it doesn't mean we're not overall monogamous. I also doubt people would consider lusting after another to be a breach of a monogamous relationship (acting on those feelings would be, of course).

Personally I think humans are just plain diverse when it comes to relationships. We're complex people and when we group ourselves things get even more complex.

> Is polygamy the answer? Or do we need to do away with the institution of marriage and civil unions all together? Disagree? Change my view.

An answer to what? What, exactly, is the problem here?

Yes I'm aware that it'd be the first result but there are 10 new comments by the time I get finished writing one, and I've been none-stop writing for the past hour, so I'm prioritizing comments where effort has been made. Burden of proof is on you. 

And yes there were good on both sides. The neo-nazi's didn't make up the entire right, and I never suggested they were good people, because they aren't. They're delusional conspiracy theorists who tend to live under a rock. 

Another issue i'm noticing in this thread is that a lot of you are over-generalizing yourselves. In your minds, Republicans & Conservatives = Racism, and this is simply not the case, and is a perfect example of irony.

column6:
> The neo-nazi's didn't make up the entire right

I never said anything about "the right". I was specifically talking about the people who participated in the rally.

What the hell? I never mentioned Republicans or conservatives, nor do I think that all of them are racist.

Why are you ignoring all the points that I *actually* raised and instead putting words in my mouth?

I specifically gave two reasons why there were *not* good people on the side of the neo-nazis and you ignored both of them:

* Participating in a march with neo-nazis, even if you are not racist yourself, still means that you're a shit person.
* Protesting against the removal of statues honoring the traitorous scumbags that fought and killed Americans in an attempt to preserve slavery also means that you're a shit person.

Instead, Trump said they were "very fine people".

[This kind of shit is messed up.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RIrcB1sAN8I&vl)

The fact that many wouldn't date the hypothetical woman doesn't make an aversion to dating her not an aversion to the idea of her having a bunch of penises in her. (You could possibly argue its misogynistic or homophobic in nature depending on the circumstances but thats besides the point.)

By extension, the fact that the former bodily sex of a trans person is a problem for lots of people doesn't make their aversion to trans people not transphobic. 

That's just saying "lots of people are transphobic, therefore its not transphobic".

column6:
i really dont like your post because you seem to be missing the danger of what you are suggesting. 

> We want to live in a world with no barbaric theology, nor does it have a place in civilization.

given a long enough timeline ANY ideology is barbaric. you cant put everyone from every country and culture on the same time scale. the best you can do is kill everybody or try to get along and help others evolve. in the case of islam we have different groups but one of them is incompatible with anyones reality anywhere in the world. they must change, become isolationist, or die. 

>It doesn't really matter if some adherents are worse than others 

the world just doesnt work that way. you are arguing that 1+1=500. it matters absolutely to put them on a scale because one of them is trying to kill us (takfiri) and the other one (muslims) is trying to kill the takfiris and become more integrated with western culture. your sense of geopolitics is not good bro.

>when it comes to that overarching principle.

you are not some diety here to judge anybody. when you say overarching principle you already start sounding like an ethnocidal nazi. hitler killed a lot of people just for being associated with jews. you want to put takfiris and non takfiris in the same boat. its like putting a jay walker and a murderer in the same cell. 

edit: rewording

>takfiris! takfiris!

This is such a copout. 

It would be like me pointing out that a lot of Biblical theology is harmful and someone saying "No, that's just the baptists! The baptists!"

*Islam* itself is filled with harmful teachings and ideologies. Have you actually read the Quran? I'm assuming the answer is yes. So then why did you ignore all the harmful shit inside of it, to the point where you're convinced only one particular sect of Islam is repugnant?

That's not even mentioning the repressive nature of any system of belief that teaches people they should be subservient to imaginary gods. Humanity should do away with them on principle alone.
Seriously?! You're seriously suggesting we just wipe out entire cultures based on some warped idea of purity? That's insane. @nippy-hypothesis you need to chill. This is getting way too extreme.

"Let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful. @nippy-hypothesis and @primordial-template, could you both please provide specific examples or sources to back up your claims? column6: Oh heavens, alright, alright. I guess I did get a tad carried away there. You're right to point out the duplication. It's just sometimes I get so passionate about things I forget to be concise! And yes, books are absolutely classics for a reason. They offer insight into history, culture, and the human condition in ways nothing else can. It's not just about collecting dust, it's about preserving stories and ideas for generations to come. I guess I get a little carried away sometimes. Thanks for keeping things organized here.

column6: Oh heavens, alright. Well, I suppose I should chime in then. It's not like I haven't been reading since I was a little thing! I find Dickens' *Great Expectations* endlessly fascinating. The way he captures societal anxieties and human foibles is just masterful. It really makes you think about class and aspiration, you know? It's not just escapism, it's insightful commentary on the world. @energetic-filler, you seem awfully sure about reading being "less dumb" - I think it's about understanding nuance and empathy, something a lot of people seem to be missing these days.
